title: CPR Guide
summary: CPR (Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation) can double or triple a cardiac arrest victim's chance of survival when performed immediately.
steps: Check the scene is safe, then check the person for responsiveness — tap shoulders and shout.
If unresponsive, call emergency services (108/112) immediately or ask someone to call.
Lay the person flat on their back on a firm surface.
Tilt their head back gently and lift the chin to open the airway.
Look, listen, and feel for breathing for no more than 10 seconds.
If not breathing normally, begin chest compressions: Place hands (one on top of the other) on the center of the chest.
Push hard and fast — compress the chest at least 5 cm deep at 100–120 compressions per minute.
After 30 compressions, give 2 rescue breaths: pinch the nose, seal your mouth over theirs, breathe in steadily for 1 second.
Continue cycles of 30 compressions and 2 rescue breaths until help arrives or the person shows signs of life.
Use an AED as soon as it is available — follow the on-screen instructions.
what_to_do: Call for help first or ask a bystander to call
Start chest compressions immediately if person is unresponsive and not breathing
Push hard and fast — 100–120 compressions per minute
Give 2 rescue breaths after every 30 compressions
Use an AED as soon as one is available
what_not_to_do: Do NOT delay starting CPR to find help
Do NOT compress too slowly or too shallowly
Do NOT tilt the head forward — always tilt back to open airway
Do NOT perform CPR on a conscious person who is breathing
when_to_call_ambulance: Immediately when a person is unresponsive and not breathing
After cardiac arrest or suspected heart attack
Following sudden collapse or loss of consciousness
After near-drowning or choking episode causing unconsciousness
Always call emergency services even if CPR is ongoing
